title: Requests, Limits, QoS, Probes, Startup, Readiness, and Resource Failure · Platform-System Defense
summary: A release review compares fresh requests, limits, qos, probes, startup, readiness, and resource failure choices and accepts only answers tied to observable API, controller, workload, network, storage, or policy evidence. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for health and resource behavior policy. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Relate CPU and memory requests to scheduling and accounting, limits to runtime enforcement, and application concurrency to measured capacity.
Derive Guaranteed, Burstable, and BestEffort QoS, container OOM, Pod eviction, node pressure, and restart evidence from effective resources.
Separate startup gating, traffic readiness, process liveness, container restart, endpoint conditions, and dependency health.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

steps.title: K8s Requests Limits Scheduling Runtime · changed case · Quiz K8s Requests Limits Scheduling Runtime
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz service passes liveness while serving stale data, has no startup probe, requests 100m but uses 1250m CPU, and is OOMKilled under pressure. Redesign probes, requests, limits, QoS, graceful termination, and changed-load checks. Competency probe: Relate CPU and memory requests to scheduling and accounting, limits to runtime enforcement, and application concurrency to measured capacity.

Which action demonstrates K8s Requests Limits Scheduling Runtime without dropping earlier contracts?
